attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with pain and swelling in her right hand. Yesterday afternoon, her cat bit her hand while she was giving it medication. The puncture wound is on the dorsum of her right hand. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is marked erythema and tenderness around the bite site with surrounding warmth and swelling. There is no crepitus or lymphangitis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely causative organism?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of an infected cat bite, with rapid onset of cellulitis within 24 hours of injury. The most common organism isolated from cat bite infections is Pasteurella multocida, which is present in the oral flora of 70-90% of cats. P. multocida infections typically develop rapidly, usually within 24 hours of the bite, causing cellulitis with marked inflammation and tenderness.

While Staphylococcus aureus can cause skin and soft tissue infections and may be present in cat bite infections, it is not the most common pathogen. It is more commonly associated with primary skin infections rather than bite wounds.

Streptococcus pyogenes can also cause cellulitis but is not typically associated with cat bites. Like S. aureus, it is more commonly associated with primary skin infections or infections through breaks in the skin.

Capnocytophaga canimorsus is more commonly associated with dog bites rather than cat bites. It typically causes severe systemic illness, particularly in immunocompromised patients or those with asplenia, which is not the case here.

Bartonella henselae is the causative organism of cat scratch disease, transmitted through cat scratches rather than bites. It typically causes a more indolent infection with regional lymphadenopathy rather than acute cellulitis.

Cat bites have a higher infection rate compared to dog bites due to their sharp teeth causing deep puncture wounds that are difficult to clean and create an ideal environment for bacterial growth. Empiric antibiotic therapy should cover P. multocida as well as other common organisms.
learningPoints: Pasteurella multocida is the most common pathogen in cat bite infections, causing rapidly progressive cellulitis within 24 hours.
Cat bites have a higher infection rate than dog bites due to their sharp teeth causing deep puncture wounds.
Early signs of cat bite infection include erythema, swelling, and tenderness developing within 24 hours of injury.
references: Dendle C, Looke D. Management of mammalian bites. Aust Fam Physician. 2009;38(11):868-874.
Greene SE, Fritz SA. Infectious Complications of Bite Injuries. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2021;35(1):219-236. doi:10.1016/j.idc.2020.10.005
